speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Tom Wahl and Elizabeth Gamble Wahl took place at their home in Pullman on April 4th, 1977. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: You won't get much yet for a while because I can't remember what, where we were or what. I think you about poked me dry. Well, I don't know about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing that I was thinking about was when you were young and growing up, how relatively well people were doing around Genesee, knowing that the area was developed earlier comparatively than the rest of the county for farming. I wondered how people were doing in the 15, 20s.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, as near as I can tell, of course my memory doesn't start quite as early as you might wish it would. I was born in 1911 as you know. But when I was about six years old, I think people, farmers, that is people that had come in early and had land and had soldering land or had stuff to sell. I mean, I didn't mean to say soldering land. I think they were fairly prosperous by the standards of those times. By that I mean my parents saw fit to buy a Ford car in about 1917, a Model T Ford car. This was their first automobile. And I suppose there was a little inflation going on too because the war was heating up a little later there. So in 1918, they traded that Ford car for a Dodge. And in 1919, they traded that Dodge for a Franklin. So you kind of see a step up in the quality of the car. However, it might point it up a little better. I did hear my father make the remark that the Dodge car, he got more for it on the trade than he had paid for it. There was no such thing as used cars, you know, and they were pretty easy item to sell. But there must have been some inflation there too. No doubt there was quite a pad in the prices. Now it seemed to me like that Franklin probably cost about like a Cadillac would cost, something like that. And I think that he gave something over $2,700.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he could pay for that in cash at the time?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, I think so. Of course, he might have had to borrow the money at a bank. But I don't think he ever bought anything on time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said inflation, which makes me think of the, he said he never would have bought anything

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: on time? No. He financed it through a bank. There, you know, pay as you go hadn't really started then either. You had credit at the bank, you had credit at the stores, and it was customary for farmers to pay the bills once a year after harvest. It was a farm-oriented economy, and the people that farmers did business with expected to carry them until after harvest. But probably he bought these things after harvest. Well, come figure, he didn't buy the Ford after harvest as he bought it early in the summer of 1917.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the prices during your time as being particularly good? See, I've heard that the times have been better.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I can't tell you what the prices for farm clothes were, but compared to what you buy, I think they probably were pretty good. Now my father told of when he first came there, and that's before there was a railroad of Genesee, they hauled their wheat to Lewiston in those days, and that would be sometime after 1880, before my time. Haul a whole load of wheat to Lewiston and only have enough money to buy a pair of cowhide boots. So there was a time before that that had been really tough, and it was really tough too, from what he has said. Now, I think in maybe 1890, and I'm guessing here a little bit, but by 1890 I think things had gotten quite a bit better. But in the year of 1893, they had a wet year, and hardly anybody harvested anything in this whole country, this whole police country down there. They didn't have the machinery to thrash with that they have now, they didn't have the machinery to handle it with. My people headed in that time, and a header, as you probably know, is a machine that just cuts wheat about as a combine does, but doesn't thrash it. You have to then put it in a stationary threshing machine or in a stack, and later in a thrashing machine. He passed up a chance, I guess, to get his thrashed. It was headed already, that is cut, and stacked, and he passed up a chance to get it thrashed because he was kind of hoping for a better deal on the thrashing. It started raining, and nobody that hadn't done it real early, nobody got their crop harvested. He said that except for the fact that you had the merchants and bankers and so forth carried them for the next year, it would have been awful tough going because they didn't have any money. Of course, they were not as dependent on money then as you were now. There was very little taxes, and very few things that you had to buy, most of the stuff you produced that you ate and canned to preserve it in one way or another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the role of J.P. Palmer at that time in Tennessee? Have you heard the stories that they had without him?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, it's all kind of hearsay as far as I'm concerned, but there's an awful lot of land passed into Palmer's hands, and I think most of it through foreclosure of mortgages. Now, what do you know about his background? How did he come in there? Can you tell me that from what you've heard?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I've heard is that it was largely through Lewiston. He came to Lewiston and offered all kinds of stories. I've heard he made quite a bit of money on reservation land. I've heard he sold whiskey to the Indians and various things like that, which may or may not be.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Did he come in there and start out running a saloon? Do you know that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Lewiston?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it's in connection with selling whiskey that I've heard about these early activities. Whether he had a saloon or not, have you heard that?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I think I heard someone make this crack, that he came in there with nothing but a bottle of whiskey and a box of cigars. So he didn't start at the top of the totem pole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By 1890, he had quite a bit of influence in towns in the river.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Now, there is this that I've heard, and I think it's true enough. He seemed to want very much to have his name perpetuated. He started, somehow or other, a number of little towns by either contributing the land or something with the stipulation that the town would be called Vollmer. And there was one over near Troy, as you know, and I think there was one or two up on the reservation. And every time he did this, so the story goes, the first act of the town meeting was to change the name of the town to something else. Now, wasn't Troy Vollmer? And I think there was one or two over in Nez Perce's town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who carried your parents at that time?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I suppose that they banked through the Vollmer Bank. That is, the bank at Genesee there, in my memory, was a branch of the Lewiston Bank. And there were two banks there within my memory. One was the Genesee Exchange Bank, and the other was the First Bank of Genesee. And I think that the First Bank of Genesee was a branch of the Vollmer Bank at Lewiston. And the Exchange Bank, I think, was locally owned. The Exchange Bank went broke, in my memory, during the Depression. The other one didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there still a hard feeling when you were growing up in the community about the Vollmer

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: and the Vollmer Bank? Oh, I think so. Now, of course, you know, farmers typically are sort of mad that they're bankers or were. But this bank, they were particularly, there was a particular amount of hard feeling between the farmers and the bank, don't you think, Liz, Vollmer Bank? Yes. Yeah. Now, there was a man named White, and his...

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: He was hardly disliked. I know that.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: S.D. White worked for J.P. Vollmer Company, and I don't know just what his duties were, but it's been referred to many times, you know, that he was sort of, oh, he did some of the unpleasant things for them. He was not an attractive looking man, but I don't think he was too bad of a man, but he was a sort of what I've heard him referred to as a scalper. And this is not anything really illegal, but let's see if I could remember this incident. Yes. He acquired a cloud on the title of a piece of property that my father owned, I guess. And it cost my dad some money to get it straightened out. And that was a sort of a way of V.S.D. White sort of making his living, I guess. And my father, although the families were friendly, still my father always held out against them a little bit. It wasn't a big thing. It wasn't a thing that you couldn't live with, but it was a thing that he resented.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it as an agent of Vollmer, do you think?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: He was in a position to do this, probably because he knew things working for Vollmer that he might not otherwise have known. But no, I don't think that the bank itself was involved in this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well Vollmer's presence continued to be felt around Genesee and long after the 93.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: If you take a book like this at me here and you go through it, you'll just see an awful lot of farmland listed in the name of J.P. Vollmer. Now, land wasn't worth much. Times have been hard. A lot of people quit when they didn't have to quit and so on. And I don't think there'd be anything illegal about it, but a lot of people felt like, particularly the farmers, seemed to feel like if he got you in a buying way, he wouldn't back off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it happened mostly through the store or through his general merchandise store and those kinds of loans or through the bank?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, my knowledge would have been more through the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had mortgages, you think, in some places? Mortgage, yeah.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And through the panics and depressions of the time, people would be dispersed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that one year was enough to set up?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, that's more than one year because I don't think prosperity started very early. I know my people had it really pretty hard through the 80s. I don't believe that their prosperity started until maybe just before 93. 93 probably wiped out what prosperity they'd had and then probably beginning with 1894. And there, as I can tell, there must have been quite a little bit of prosperity then from about 1895 on up into and through the 20s. At least there were better houses, you know, and of course there were lots of things to spend money for too, fences and everything else, machine raid was the beginning of the mechanization of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Rosenstein store?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, my parents liked the Rosenstein's very, very well. And of course, like all the other merchants, I think they put out a credit for a year. I don't think anybody did cash business with a farmer. My dad, when the store started billing you every month, he resented it hardly. It just wasn't cricket at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the Rosenstein's at all?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, I can remember them. I like to go in there too because if I went in there my parents, they always gave me a cookie. They owned some land right close to where we farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much do you think they were a part of the going job in the community, being Jewish? Do you think that they were somewhat set apart?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I don't know. I suppose, people being as they are, there always was discrimination by some people. Of almost any minority that you'd care to name. But it was common knowledge that the Rosenstein's were Jewish. And most people seem to think quite highly of them. They weren't the only Jewish people there. Now, I don't know when the case books came, I understood they were Jewish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Levi's I've heard.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, I didn't know them, but I've heard the name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of an influence on the development of the area do you think that the opening of the reservation land was in 95? And in terms of the traditions that you've heard about it.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I couldn't tell you, I just don't know. My parents bought Indian land later. Really, historically, I don't know too much about what the opening of the reservation entailed. But a lot of Indians sold their land, you know, patented their land and sold it in order to get the money. A lot of them didn't. There's a lot of land still held by Indians too in the reservation. I suppose that much fine farmland being opened to Indians, it probably was being opened for farming, probably made quite a difference. I know a lot of people that were established over near Moscow, well, I shouldn't say I know a lot of people, but some people, went over and homesteaded over in the Kansas prairie area. Now, I don't know how much of that had been reservation land. What did they do? They opened the reservation to homesteading?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, in 95. First they distributed the land to those Indians, the first that were there. I don't know what exactly the amount was, but after they had given them a certain allotment, then they threw the whole thing out. I see. I see. I see for a second what was left. I've seen the old Luceman Tribune, the day that it opened, and it would stand deep.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, that probably explains how come that I had an uncle that homesteaded over near Herdland. Now, my father, you understand, never homesteaded. His father homesteaded, and all of his brothers and sisters homesteaded, someplace or other, but he didn't. Of course, he was the oldest surviving son after his brother died, and it was necessary for one mature man to be sort of in charge of the father's affairs. Actually, there was the widow and all the kids to take care of, and he was the oldest, and there was, I think, 12 kids. I see my dad was 10 years old, I guess, when they came there. He was born in 71, and he was about 20 years old when his father died.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: He was 18. 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he took over the family?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, his brother, he had a brother that was a couple years older, but he got killed a couple years later, I guess.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: He was 18, I believe, when his brother was around.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I can find out.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: He was born in 1870. No, that was not really the reason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they used that as an excuse. Yes. Now, I knew that story that they said that they couldn't say Baltimore, but I didn't know that was the excuse that they gave.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, they said, we can't say your name right, we call it Walmer and Soul.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Now, my dad was born in 1870, February 5, 1870. His father died in 1887, leaving John, the oldest son, in charge of the place there. But John died in 1893, so my father would have been, at that time, from 70 to 93, he would have been 23 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have any brothers and sisters in the family?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: They, of course, then would have all been younger, and one of them was an epileptic, and that put the others quite a bit younger, and quite a few of them are girls. But all of them, I think, with the possible exception of the epileptic, homesteaded some land, some white land. And then two of them went to college, which my father helped them do, helped them go to college, and he always felt like he had given up his chance for an education by doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he cause him to do away his marriage at all for the need to give up?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, that, see, my dad didn't marry until...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: 1907. So, that would have made him 37 years old, wouldn't it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think, I think, I wonder if that was the child that was epileptic. I wonder if that was the one that Mary Edwards mentioned to me.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: She probably knew about it, but I doubt if she knew him. He was... No, I don't have the date of his death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your mother or your grandmother that she adored so much?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: My mother. She's probably talking about my brother, right? Cripple, but he wasn't epileptic, he was spastic.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: He was spastic. Tom's brother was spastic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She told me that she spoke so highly of your mother. Yes, she just adored her.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, my mother taught a great deal of all of the great children, too. Of course, my mother was a young maker in the community and they were the kids, you know, of her friends. Of friends that were older, of course, than my people. And it was, they were our closest neighbors, Mary and her brothers. And, well, her brothers, at least some of them, always sort of started their first job working for my dad. And this young couple, I think they were quite pleased to have these nice, intelligent young kids in their house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm sure she's told you how she would love to come over and play the piano with her mother.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Mother was a teacher, you know, and of course, and I think my instinct to teach her, too. Don't you, Liz? Oh, yes. Pretty good teacher. She couldn't help but try to teach anybody that came within her reach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that would help explain why she said she'd rather been at your house than, you know, anywhere.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Uh-huh, probably.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: And Tom's mother taught her how to sew.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: And Mary and her sister and her brothers, of course, they were all older than I was, but they were always real nice to me, you know. Older kids can make it kind of miserable for a younger kid or they can make it pretty nice. Well, they made it pretty nice for me, and one of the nice things that they did, these kids had an awful lot of freedom, even for these kind of times. And one thing they had was a coaster wagon and an old gray horse, and they hooked a single piece of this horse to this little kid's wagon and dragged it back and forth from... ...or south of us, or the cooking was going on at their place about three-quarters of a mile north of us. So every day, maybe several times a day, these kids would make this trip with an old gray horse and a kid's wagon. So I got to ride in this wagon once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We see the cooking was going on.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, wherever they were working, you know, probably their mother would be cooking the meals at one place or the other. Because, you know, with horses, you don't jump in the car and drive from where you feed the horses. You don't go home every time if you're working in a field this far away where you can't arrange to live there somehow or other. I think really, how came there were these too far? Well, now, the gray homestead was down there, and then one of the gray boys, when he got older, bought a place between the homestead and our place. And then there was the land to the north of us. I don't know how they acquired it, but ever as far back as my memory reaches, and before that, they had this land on both sides of us. Well, the average farm that wasn't split, though, you wouldn't have to be doing that. You would have to be cooking out in the field, would you? Or would people still do that? Well, I was about three or four miles apart, and there was a barn, and my father built a house and a barn. This was on the reservation, three or four miles from where his home was. And when they were farming down there, they just simply moved down there. Or sometimes his brother would farm down there, and one of his sisters would cook down at this other place. Later, he rented it. They kind of split up, and they had two outfits to farm with then, and it wasn't necessary to make all this moving back and forth. No, with horses, when you moved down to one farm, you moved your horses there, and you stayed there and took care of them. And you couldn't quickly move from one place to the other. It might take you way off to go down four or five miles and get your horses hooked up and do any work and get unhooked. With horses, you can't just take your lunch either. You've got to stop and feed the horses. It takes you an hour and a half for noon. You know that you're working, but the horses aren't. You come in about 1130, and you've got all those horses to feed before you get your food. And then you've got to hook them up again, see if they're watered. At night, you've got to feed them when you bring them in from the field and unharnass them. And after supper, you've got to go out and feed them again. And in the morning, you've got to get up and harness them and feed them. And then after breakfast, you've got to go out and hook them to the implementer. It's a lot different than it is from running a tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I tend to forget because it's a lot more than I do.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: So, you can't... Well, just to give you an idea, now my father didn't drive particularly hard and didn't work with men particularly hard by the standards of those times. But even as a kid, if I was running a team for him, I had to get up at... 530 would be the latest. And you'd go out and feed and harness your horses and clean out your barn. There's all that manure too, you know. It's got to be fished out. And then come back in and eat and then go back out and get them strung out. And you were expected to be in the field with that team by 7, not 8 or 9, but 7 or 5. Or the very latest 7 or 30. But when you consider that you've got to stop by about 1130, you don't get an awful lot of plowing done. And when you consider that the outfit that one man drove usually consisted of two 14 inch plows and 8 head of horses. And compare that with what a tractor can do... ...plowing 160 acres. You spent all summer near it, it seemed like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would mean then a good long period of time at the edifice when you were down there?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, you'd be well farming on what you would do then. You did what farming you could do and you got done what you could do. And it depended on the weather as it does now, but more so. And in the spring, as early as you possibly could, you probably started plowing. And if you could get some of that work down and seeded, if you felt like you had to, you might. But most likely you would seed ground that you had plowed the previous fall if you were going to put in any spring crop. But we, in my youth, very little spring crops that we put in, usually started this way. You somehow or other got your land plowed and you summer fouled it that summer, worked it, you know, kept the weeds down. And then seeded it in the fall, the fall of wheat. Say half your place. And the other half presumably had been seeded the fall before. Now if you didn't get it seeded in the fall, you might then have to scurry around and try to seed it in the spring. Or if you had a year that gave you a little bit of leeway, you might seed some of the land that you plowed in the fall. You might work it up and seed it in the spring and have a spring crop and a fall crop. And then you might follow a rotation that worked somewhat like this, a fall crop, a spring crop, and a year idle for summer fowl. But that was pretty much the way it went. And then you probably had some land, well you had to have some land in hay of some kind or another. The meadows on the lowland would be in Timothy, you had the hay to put up. And you might have some alfalfa on some land that needed the alfalfa for the land and you needed the alfalfa for the hay. You had some cattle, almost everybody had some cattle, enough for their own meat and enough for their own milk. And maybe sell some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was fallow usually a clover or something like that?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Fallow means to leave it black and bare and work it to preserve the moisture and kill the weeds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say up to half of the place you would...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, that was the rotation my people followed most of the time. It was half summer fowl and half wheat. That is of the land that you plowed. The alfalfa of course is a perennial legume and you didn't plow it up until it had been in a number of years and wasn't producing hay very well. Then you might put alfalfa on another piece of ground and break up that alfalfa ground and plant a grain crop on it. Because it would produce pretty well, you know, following that rest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they knew from experience that that was the best kind of rotation to fowl like that? Or do you think it was more an old... Old?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Old belief. For the time I think it was the only thing they could do. That was sod when they came here, you know. And it's a battle to grow stuff on sod and so you break the ground down as fast as you can with this vegetative vegetable material and the land was fertile. And so you decompose that vegetative vegetable material in the soil. Well, I think that it's breaking up new country. I think that you almost would have to do that. Not now, not maybe a desert, but this bunchgrass country. Not only was there this bunchgrass, years and years and years of the bunchgrass in various stages of decay, but also the brush and so on that was mixed in with bunchgrass. It was quite a job to break up a piece of ground. That was one of the things that the homesteaders had to face. They probably made the years from 1880 to 1890 kind of tough. It was pretty hard to get enough stuff to grow to support a family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that it was hard to break the bunchgrass sod.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, you dang right it was hard. You didn't plow that with two bottoms, you plowed that with one foot burner, a sod buster. Have you ever seen one? Yeah. Now you kind of know what...that's hard work. I haven't done it. I haven't done any of it. Elizabeth's brothers broke up some land in recent years in my memory. Her brother John broke two ribs running this foot burner and he was a real strong, husky man. Because when the point of this foot burner hits a root and you don't happen to have over the handle right and they hit you in the side, it's darn easy to break a rib. And what do you plow in a day? An acre? I don't know. Not very much. Well, you could figure that out, how much you plow in a day. An acre of ground is a strip of ground a half a mile long and 16 and a half feet wide. And this plow would probably take maybe anywhere from 12 to 16 inches depending on the size of plow you used. But say that it was a 12 inch plow for breaking a sod, that'd be 8 and a quarter miles, you'd have to hold the handles of that thing and at the same time drive the team and take care of the team. And probably milk cows after you got them taken care of. And you walked of course. The horses pulled the plow but you had to keep it upright and you had to guide the horses and you had to guide the plow. And pretty rough days worked by the time you walked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think you had to cut brush very much? I mean, did you get tanned?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: If there was brush you would have to at least get it to the shape that you could get plowed through it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, when I've heard people talk in other parts of the county about the 90s when their parents first homesteaded, I repeatedly hear that the parents father went to Genesee during the harvest in the 90s and that would be the only money that he'd be able to bring home for the year. He'd be down and stay there for the whole season. Clearly compared to the other parts of the county that were in the timber country, Genesee had not only land broken up by that time but they must have had money too to be able to pay for the thrashing that these men were doing.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, well, I think that they started out with very little money and I think they went on credit, all of them looking forward and realizing that there wasn't any harvest, nobody was going to make it. The merchant or anybody else. Now, a lot of people, or some people I should say, probably came in there with more money than others. You didn't go homesteading with nothing but your bare hands, you had to have something behind you. You had to have an outfit or something that didn't take much of an outfit. You might catch some of the Indian horses and break them. And probably, well, just to show you what little bit they would put in an outfit of course, they'd have to have a plow. Hopefully they'd have a harrow. But I heard of them using some of the brush they'd cut off of the place to harrow it with, harrow it with thorn brush. Drag the brush around on top of this sod that you broke up to try to smooth it off and work it down to some semi-seeds. Of course the soil was just abundantly fertile and you could get away with murder as far as the way you treated the soil was concerned. You didn't have enough power to do much harm to the soil anyway, not like a tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to have, was it enough to have a breaking plow, you didn't have to have another plow too?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: You couldn't use the other plow until after you'd got it broken up and used, worked it. I imagine you'd use a breaking plow for several years. I can't really say for sure, but the second time you plowed it still wouldn't plow like you'd want to, I'm sure of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what would be the next plow that everybody would use?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, what we'd call a gang plow or a sulky. We used gang plow. Sulky would be a plow with one bottom that you rode on, gang plow has two bottoms or more. But there was an awful lot of foot burners, as they called them, that were like a saw breaker, excepting that the mow board was more abrupt. Usually they were a bigger plow and a more abrupt mow board share. Well, by abrupt I mean the angle between the mow board and the land side would be larger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else for the outfit? The minimum outfit that you're talking about.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: The minimum outfit would be two harnesses. A sod buster is some kind of a plow. If you bought up land that had already been broken out, maybe you had a better plow than that, or access to one. And I imagine that there was homesteads started with nothing more than some horses and harness and plow. You'd have a wagon of some sort or you never could have gotten there, probably, although I had an aunt whose husband walked up from Riparia and he brought everything he brought in on his back. And lived in a dugout out here near Johnson his first winter. He bought a relinquishment, I believe, that had a shack on it. The shack fell down, but he continued to live in a hole that he dug and covered with a shack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was not that unusual to have a dugout? And there were people that had them?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, it was a little bit unusual because in proving up on a homestead you had to make certain improvements. And usually one of the first improvements they made was build a house of some kind. Now these houses usually, I've seen some of them, were usually one room with an open attic. And the boards on the outside were vertical boards with the cracks covered with battens, about a 12 inch board covered with about a 4 inch batt on the outside. And then maybe three horizontal 2x4s inside the house that those boards were nailed to. And the one that I saw was on our house, we used it for a bunkhouse for quite a while. The rafters were split rails. And it had a ceiling in it. And the ceiling joists were split rails when boards were nailed to it. I suppose it kind of smoothed off as best they could in the past.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were log canyons real rare?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Down here they were because there weren't logs. That is, by down here I mean in Genesee. There were a few logs in some of the canyons and so forth. But pretty early they got lumber. Most of those houses were lumber or used some lumber. I think that probably, excuse me, that probably there were some sawmills in, during the mining days maybe. Because in this part of the country, I don't know of any log houses that the settlers had. They got lumber somewhere, shipped it in. But you see there was boat traffic up the river. And there wasn't very long until there was a railroad came to Colfax. And quite a while before that miners had been building buildings and sluice boxes and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a mill on the foothills of Moscow Mountain. Barton had a sawmill, the guy that had that early hotel in Moscow. That's the earliest sawmill.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Do you know how, when?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I think it was the 70s.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, a lot of these people came in, must have, 70 would have been awfully early to be homesteading. Most of them that I know, well any, I don't know of anybody homesteading before the 70s. And most of them seemed to have come in around Genesee there in the rather late 70s or early 80s. My grandfather must have known and must have had his place located before then because, you know, a year before then because he spent the winter, the fall of 79 down here at Johnson and didn't move his family up to the homestead until 1880. I think that there were several of the neighbors in there yet. And possibly not all the land was taken up then even then. I can still show you, I don't know, I was going to say I can still show you one pretty early house. The one that Liz and I first lived in is still standing, it's built with square nails yet. It's pretty early. Her mother's house is still standing and it's pretty early. It has square nails and some rough lumber but some pretty high quality lumber in them too. Both of them have some beaded larch. See what her wings go there. Something like that. My dad tells a story of an early day pioneer there that came in and built a brick hill and burned bricks and built a house of bricks that he made right there out of his own dirt on his own farm. And it's not very far from where he farmed but I couldn't point out the location but just a year or so ago I found one of those bricks, or one that I assumed to be one of those bricks, because my dad, we were out plowing one day and turned up one of these bricks and he told me the story then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any idea where that man came from?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I have a, I suppose that he had, this was his trade, you know a lot of people had a trade. And he may very well have sold some of these bricks, I don't know. Pretty early in the game there was a man named Herbert that was burning bricks and tile down at Uniontown then. But I don't know how early.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The market in chimneys for people's farms.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, there'd always be a market for brick alright. I've seen some early houses up in this country that are made of rock and stone and so on. I have seen some shelters later than that that were used by Chinese I guess when a railroad was built down in Lewiston there, kind of an oval, piled up like an igloo shape out of rock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Down by Lewiston? Yeah. Well for the, getting by with the minimum in these early days, what do you know about what people ate and what food?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I'll tell you one thing that Earl Clyde told me that his people had it real tough the first, I think it was the first winter, but I can't name the date for you. But his people had a haystack up the draw from where their house is in Wurlalo and so on. And Earl has told me that his parents told him that they didn't think they'd have made it that winter if it hadn't been for the prairie chickens that they were able to kill at that haystack. My dad has told me about out, go out in the early part of the year and gather a hat full of prairie chicken eggs so I know they ate them, kind of considered it a treat. But of course, I suppose all pioneers had, as soon as they could or whenever they had the opportunity, butchered and cured some pork. Because it's the meat that apparently every one of them knew how to cure. All of my early childhood, the most big part of the winter was you butchered some pigs and then you took care of that meat and you salted it and then you smoked it. And then that lasted us all through summer. We didn't buy much meat in the summertime. There wasn't much opportunity to buy meat in the summertime. You had chickens because you could not only eat the eggs but you could, a family could eat a chicken or two before they would spoil. But there was no refrigeration. If there were a few more people around you might arrange to get a sheep and butcher it sometime. But you had to eat something that was small enough that you could eat it before it all started. You might kill a small calf or a youngish calf and divide it up among the neighbors to have summer meat. You know, like if there was a crew or during harvest time, it was not at all uncommon in my memory in harvest time that several of the neighbors would get together and they'd decide, well I'll take a quarter and I'll take a quarter and I'll take a quarter. And maybe among four families with their hired men and the harvesters that you'd have extra, well you could eat this up before it spoiled. But there's been lots of meat spoiled too that I've seen. Lots of times in the summer if there was people with sheep that would come through looking for sheep pasture, why you'd arrange to get a sheep because you could butcher that and eat it up pretty fast. They were real good too, real welcome change from bacon and ham. And they canned lots of meat without pressure cookers either and a lot of people didn't die. My mother canned meat all the time. A lot of the choice meat like the tenderloin from a pig, you'd can it rather than cure it. That's the same cut that you make Canadian bacon or if it's around eye, it comes along the side of the backbone. We'd eat it now for pork chops and sell the people the bone instead of just eating the meat. But we threw the bones away at camp and we ate what we couldn't get off the bones. We ate it then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your big meal at lunch time?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No. Well yes, yes. Your new meal was your big meal. Although you ate three, that's one thing, we ate three pretty nice meals. But very few of us got fat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was breakfast a big meal?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: You get up at 5.30 and feed and curry and harness eight horses and maybe milk two cows and then come in and eat breakfast. Well you'll eat a pretty good breakfast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would it be usually for breakfast?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I still eat this kind of breakfast. Well I can't now but I would like to. You'd have eggs or bacon or ham or a combination of two of them. Potatoes, always potatoes. Bread or toast or hot cakes. Milk and coffee. Well you might have some mush, a bowl of mush along with it. You ate all you wanted of it, just when you got full you ate it. If you wanted three eggs you ate three eggs. If you wanted four you ate four. You didn't want as much bacon or ham as you'd want now though. And for this reason there wasn't any water added to your ham. And you didn't have to put salt on it to make it salty enough. Because it was salty enough that it would last for four months in the heat of the summer. And by the time you're ready to butcher the next hog, well you're lucky if you could even cut that bacon or ham. And one of the things that you almost always did if you're preparing bacon is slice it and boil it and then fry it. Or parboil it to get some of the salt out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was supper very much like dinner?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Pretty much. Usually you didn't have so much fried stuff. You had your fried things at breakfast. And then by dinner and suppertime. That's what we called it. Breakfast, dinner, and supper. We never ate dinner at night. We ate supper at night. And it would be... You left the field at six and you got in and probably ate your supper around 6.30 or something like that. Maybe a little after six. And then you went out and did your chores and then you came in and went to bed. So you ate a pretty good supper and you worked part of it off doing your chores. You went to bed and you usually went right to sleep. Morning came awful early. Even though you went to bed early.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean there wasn't much socializing at night after...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: There was no radio.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I mean, just the family.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh yes, you enjoyed a little of the evening. My dad always went to bed pretty early. My mother would sit up for a while. And in the wintertime, that's when you did your socializing. But, oh, in the long summer evenings, you know, if there's a bunch of hired men around, you know, maybe some evening you'd all take the notion to... Maybe somebody could play a harmonica or sing or something. This is what you did. It was real interesting. Real fun. And, oh, we read. Sometimes lots. Mostly to ourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of books? Just out of curiosity. Just curious, the kind of reading that you would do when you were a kid. Was it Rover Boys that were classics?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: The first book that I read was The Man on the Box. It was a dime novel sort of a romance. I don't know if I have a copy of it yet. I kind of kept it for the... It was kind of a love story. That one was. I can't know what else I read. Of course, I read... I read everything I could get my hands on. So there wasn't any... I was a reader. I learned to read early and it was easy for me. And I just read everything. If there wasn't anything else left to read, I read The Seer's Robot Catalog or The Label on the Bacon Cider can. It didn't make much difference. But we had a set of books of knowledge and it was just almost like that. It was a little older set than this. We picked this up since. And it just fascinated me. I take a volume at random and you can read... You can use them like an encyclopedia or you can just read through them and you find something that interests you every page you turn to. There's a real privilege I had that a lot of kids didn't have. Most kids didn't have. But my father bought these. My mother and father bought these and his brother bought some for my cousins. So I had just from those a pretty good background.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In school, were they using the guffy?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No, not in my time. It was free and tried well on Siersen and Martin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Studies in the region. Siersen and Martin. Well, do you think that when you were in school that... There was a first reader that was used when I was in school. It was not mine, but it was the one I liked. When you were in school as a youngster, do you think that you're interested in the readings that you part from many or most of the boys in school? I had a feeling that from the little that I heard of it, an awful lot of kids were particularly excited by the reading part of the school.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, yes, I know a lot of kids had a lot more difficulty reading than I did. But there's an awful lot of them that developed a skill too. One of the nice things about the one room country school was that the first grader got to listen to all the other seven grades recite and read aloud. And of course the old kids got to listen to the little kids make their first feeble attempts at reading and spelling. And there's quite a bit of sympathy too. And of course an awful lot of these kids, the oldest kid was related, probably a brother, to the littlest kid. And a bunch of them in between. There's something that is too bad they've missed. This continual, this ability to see where you're going and also where you've come from. And all of the review of all the stuff that you ever learned for eight years. By the time you're ready to finish the eighth grade you pretty well know what the first graders are doing. And you've also heard eight years of eighth graders recite if you count your own too. So we knew that by the time you're in the second grade you knew pretty much what you were going to do in the second grade. It's a learning process I think it would. I don't think they, with their kindergarten and everything else, I don't think they're anywhere near to as good a system as this one of where you get to hear the old kids. The old kids hear the little ones and they guide each other and help each other and fight with each other and keep order. I don't believe they have anything that equals it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can see that in pioneering times is that especially it seems for the boys when there was any important work to be done at home on the farm that there was no question about it.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: The generation before me I think that was quite true. Now my dad only went to the fifth grade I think. He did manage to go to some private schools on his own later. But I think about fifth grade was the limit of his formal schooling. He had some place or other picked up algebra. He could write a real good letter and he could read anything. But he had the advantage though that his father was an educated man. His father had a degree in engineering, civil engineering, which he acquired in Germany. So that's quite an advantage to kids. Now the Gamble kids, their parents were both educated people. And although the boys from the Gamble family didn't go very far in school, still they were pretty well educated if they hadn't gone to school at all having parents like that. His father was a minister and his mother was a school teacher. And she was a remarkably well educated woman. Now when I was a student in college, Mrs. Gamble, Lola's and Elizabeth's mother, she could sit down and derive the formula for taking the square root of a number by using the binomial theorem, which I had gone through but I couldn't do it now. After she'd had a chance to forget it for 20 years or 30, she was able to sit right down and go right through. That's rather unusual for anyone, particularly you'd think for a woman teacher. But she was pretty smart and had a pretty good education. She was good enough at describing and getting the meaning from a book that I've seen Elizabeth. If she had to make a book report, give her mother the book to read and her mother tells her what's in it and Elizabeth gets an A on the book report. So there's quite a bit of ability in the mother as well as the daughter to be able to do that. Well do you think that the way that the system would work then, that it favored the girls going on in school rather than in class? I went three years to college and my father died so then I quit. Sort of parallel roughly to his. I'd been out of school for over 13 years before I went back and started again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went back then and graduated? I mean, you ran the farm then?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Uh huh. With my mother. But my sister, as you say, as you pointed out, my sister went on to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about in your father's family? Were the girls going on further than the boys in his family?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes. One girl, one of the older girls, took nurses training up here at Spokane and a younger one graduated from the University of Idaho and her twin brother didn't graduate, although he went for a while. There were so many opportunities without going to school for a man. There was cheap land to be had, employment, and a school teacher was more or less what a girl did. Although the previous time, now my grandfather, being an educated man, one of the first things he did to sort of augment his income here was to teach the country school. And farm at the same time. But he had a big family of boys who did most of the work. Well, I suppose he did a lot of the work too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I seem to remember you saying to me at one time that I met you at Lola's house that in your generation a man teaching, a man was looked on as being kind of strange.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, I think that's true. He was kind of a sissy or something. You didn't see any men teachers hardly at all. But when I got to high school, there were lots of men taught in high school. They coached, of course, and they're very much like now. But they coached, they taught manual training, they taught math. Well, not all together, no. Some of them taught mathematics. But my first two math teachers were women in high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the grade school, you would say was...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, I never went to a grade school that was taught by a man. Although my father did. And Elizabeth's brothers did. I think there was just a change at that time. Previously it was school masters. All your Eastern history seems to be school masters. But out here it was school moms. Have you ever read Wister's book, The Virginian?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but I know of the book.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: There was a school mom in that. All that seemed like lots, most of the Western schools must have been taught by women. At least all that I went to were. It was an opportunity for a girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And a lot of girls really were determined to take advantage of it.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yes, I think so. And then it was necessary too. There was times where, by our standards, times were tough. And this was employment for women. The family made quite a sacrifice to send these girls to school. And the girls wanted to get to teaching as quick as they could. And most of them, you didn't have to have a college degree to teach. You went to normal school and got a certificate, maybe a temporary certificate, and you taught a while. And then maybe you went back to college a while and then you taught a while. Now by the time I was going to college, that was depression kits then, a lot of men got into this too, the teaching too, because it was an opportunity for them. And then, previous to that time, there was always, the men were all trying to get a farm of their own. That is the countrymen, this is what they were going to do, get an outfit and get a farm. And, didn't give you, well it was more attractive than teaching. Your chances were better. The girl, of course, teaching was a stepping stone. And then you got out in the country and met these young men and you married one of them. That happened over and over and over. That's how my mother met my father. She boarded their house part of the time. And, not at all in the town. A little country school, the closest one to us, I think a couple of those school moms married local farm-goers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of turnover then.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah. I never had, in the grades, I never had the same teacher for two consecutive years. New teacher every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that marriage every year?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No. They had a year's experience after they taught once. And a lot of them then moved on to a better salary someplace. Oh, this wasn't really typical. There's lots of schools where they hired the same teacher over and over. But, might be a lot of reasons why you'd get a different teacher. Maybe she'd go back to school again. Maybe she only had a temporary certificate, maybe she had to go back to school. I don't know. Liz could tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How tough would a community be beyond a teacher? Would a community judge her pretty?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, yes. I think sometimes they made it real tough for her and sometimes she made it tough for them. And the teacher was looked at pretty closely. She was the stranger in the community. She was the pillar of knowledge, of course. You looked at her pretty critically. And of course there's the same jealousy between parents about their kids, that they're looking for injustice and all that and so forth. And I think more people are people. They weren't any better then maybe than they are now. They probably were. And then of course too, there wasn't any radio. There was nothing to take your mind off of what was going on at the schoolhouse. A thing that maybe we should touch on, the schoolhouse in a country district, school district. This was the center of the community. Everything that happened in the community centered around the schoolhouse. If you had Sunday school, it happened at the schoolhouse. If you had a dance, it happened at the schoolhouse. If you had a party, a neighborhood party, it was at the schoolhouse. The schoolhouse, as soon as they consolidated the schools, you destroyed your community feeling. Now we used to every Christmas, way, way back then, I can remember we had dinner at the schoolhouse, party at the schoolhouse. That is since anybody whose kids were old enough to go to school and most of them, well it took in a lot of other people too. Some communities had a thing against dancing and wouldn't dance. Some of them had a thing against hard playing and wouldn't play cards. We're talking about the country school. I wish you'd get in on this.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I wish that was Thanksgiving and not Christmas.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, we had many of each. Many of times it was a program and we ate afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was asking him about how close the school looked at the teacher. He was saying that they looked at her pretty carefully with a pretty critical eye at what she did.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: That would be the community? Yes, I think so. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm wondering, it seems like since it was all so close it would be pretty easy for them. He was saying that they only stayed like a year in his school.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, I know of a school where teachers stayed six years, one right after another, six years. But that's unusual. Sometimes they stay two and three years. Then they like to go on to what they thought was a better job and others just moving to go different places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the kind of thing that would get a teacher on the wrong side in her community? It would make them not want her to come back, say, the next year?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, maybe poor discipline. The farmers were great to want their children disciplined. If the children went too wild in the school, then that might be one reason. That would be about the only thing, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was wild tame to what we would say wild is now?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I think so. I think so. Yes, it would be no drinking and no use of marijuana or anything like that. Nothing like that was going on in the schools, and I know of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for a teacher, if the kids talked back to her, would that be a lack of discipline if that happened in the classroom?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: If there was too much of it, I think. But also, they didn't like lots of noise. The noisy classrooms that they have now, those that have been tolerated at all, were quiet, and children sitting quietly and studying. A little busy sound, you know, but not lots of noise at all. And there wouldn't be so much of this having pets in the schoolhouse and having goldfish and things like that. Nothing near as much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the way she conducted herself in the community, her morals and that kind of thing?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Oh yes, that would be watched very carefully. That would be watched very carefully. But she was not expected to come out to all the little gatherings at people's homes, like the card games and things like that. They didn't expect to see the teachers there. But yes, she'd have to behave herself pretty well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean she wasn't supposed to come, or they didn't make her come?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: She wasn't really, they didn't make her come, they didn't expect her to come, and she could come if she wanted to. But that was more for the older families, and the teacher just kept to her room and worked and corrected her papers and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could she openly court, be courted while she was teaching? Is that okay? Or was that frowned on?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Rather frowned on, I think. Yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that, the funny thing about that is that, from what people told me, it seems like most of the, so many of the teachers met their husbands right while they were teaching.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: That's right, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But does that mean they weren't, that they didn't feel free to go out on a date with their home?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I don't think they felt very free, no, no. They might pursue these dates maybe in vacation time, yeah, something more like that, but not when she was busy with her schoolwork. Of course, there were some married teachers, too, quite a few married ladies teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Young married teachers or older?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, fairly young, fairly young, in their 30s maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what's this, I, you know, I have heard that the young teacher, when she got married, had to expect to have to give up her job. Now, is that true? I've heard that a lot of these, it was written in the contract that a teacher couldn't get.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: There was a time through the Depression when only a man or his wife could have a job, and they both were employed. That wouldn't do, because it was so hard to get jobs, and either the man could stay home and take care of the children and the wife have employment, or vice versa.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But before that, you said it would be okay for a woman to keep teaching after she got married. It seemed...

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, yes, and after, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that after you were married, keep teaching?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I taught one year after I was married, but I had stopped teaching for a good many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before that?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, and then I taught school one year, taught high school, after I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because Lola quit when she got married. You quit when you got married. Right. How come? The way they keep teaching.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, the farm was a pretty busy place, and there was a lot to do. And, see, she had four children right soon, and I had one child, and I had to leave it for my mother-in-law to take care of while I went and taught. There wasn't all this provision for babysitters in the good old days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I don't mean to just keep asking you about the higher teaching, but it does seem to me, I read it as what I've heard, as kind of being the main opportunity that young women had in those days to really get out there and take a leadership role. It seemed like there was not that many other choices that were open to them.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: True, there were not so many other choices. No, no. You had to go on and have a lot more education. You could teach by going to normal school just a short time, a year or so. Then you could go off and teach for three years. Then you could go back for a year, and from a normal school you graduated with just two years of work. Now you see you have to go four and five years before you're allowed to teach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that mean as far as the youngest age that women could teach at in those days? How young could they start at?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, I believe that in later years it was required that you should be 18 years old on or before the middle of October. Before that you could teach any time you got to Chittenden because there was a lady who taught down in the Genesee area years ago. She was 15 years old. That was the youngest teacher they ever had at Pine Grove School.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that would be you go through eighth grade, then you could go one year to the lowest school, normal.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, in those days, that would be before I was teaching and even before I was going to school, you could finish the eighth grade, or not even finish the eighth grade, but you were given a teacher's examination. If you passed that teacher's examination, then you could go ahead and teach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could do that without even a year at the normal school?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, with no time at all at any normal school. The first teacher that my brothers had, she had finished the eighth grade and she had taken her examination. She was 18 years old and she taught and did a very nice job. She loved the children, she was real good to the children, and she did a very fine job, although she was inexperienced. She was very young. Another teacher that my brothers had was a man, and I don't know that he had ever finished grade school, but he passed his examination and went out and taught school and did pretty well, I think. He was a good singer and he led them in singing and taught them all the old hymns and things that they wouldn't allow to be sung in school. Now, you see, you can't sing hymns, you can't even read a little out of the Bible anymore. But this man taught them the hymns and sang away there and taught them many other things, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tom told me that he remembers the parents as being jealous about, you know, looking to make sure that the teacher didn't favor one kid over another.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Oh yes, that would make trouble. That would make trouble in a school. If teachers really were, most that I ever knew were very fair and tried to be very fair, but some parents were looking and feeling that their children were not treated quite right. That was very bad for the teachers, very bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How forward were parents about those kinds of objections in those days? Did they feel free to come in and give the teacher a dressing down?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, they really didn't. They just talked something about it. They might sometimes talk to the directors of the school and complain about it, not very often. Sometimes they would write a note to the teacher telling them what was wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you mean a grievance, saying, you're not treating my child well enough?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, you know, say something that some other child had been favored. But I had a very nice note once when I was teaching at country school. This man had children in my school and he wrote saying, I know a teacher may get many notes when things are going wrong, but I want you to get this note when everything is going just right. He had been a school teacher himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that's the exception more than a word.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, and I think that anyone who has ever taught at country school, there would be more understanding, you know, telling you that everything was going just right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that's how they would express a grievance, with the kid just blowing a note.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Probably a note. Sometimes it would come to the place where the teacher would be boarding and say a few things, but often it would be to the directors, complaining to the directors. But all the farm people were pretty busy and if things were going at all smoothly at school, they were happy and happy to have the children out from underfoot and down where somebody was teaching them something and keeping a pretty firm hand and pretty much disciplined.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would mothers come to the class to see what school was like in the country schools? I've heard about that in town, but I'm wondering if it happened to come to...

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I had a lady who visited my school, but she had been a teacher herself and that is why she was interested in visiting the schools. A very pleasant visitor, a very helpful visitor. I remember her coming in. Once in a while some mothers would come to visit. Always had something kind to say. And whether they were the mothers of children that I went to school with or the mothers of children I taught. They were always very nice in the school room. We had a man teacher who taught us one year when I went to Mount Tomer and he established discipline the year he was there. He did some quipping and he kept people after school for whispering and talking when they weren't supposed to. And he was very, very much liked by all the community because they were happy to have this good discipline. And he had been receiving the wage of $75 a month, but when he came to the end of his term the school board offered him $100 a month if he would just stay with them. And that was just unheard of. $100 a month was just unheard of. And he told them, life is too short.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he didn't stay?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No. No, he didn't stay for the $100.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do the kids think about it? Were they very happy with his methods?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, kind of this way, they didn't like it so well when he was with them. But as they looked back upon it the next year I would hear them tell him what a fine teacher this man had been. And the thing they liked about him was the severe discipline made everybody mind and toe the mark.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the kids actually appreciate that?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: They did. And of course they'd hear at home what a good teacher this was and didn't allow any nonsense around there. They'd hear this at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would women teachers be as strict as he was?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: The women teachers that I had were not as strict as he. No, they were not. They all did some spanking but they were not as strict as this man teacher was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your generation of teachers believe in spanking and corporal punishment too?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Not so much. No. Not nearly so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder what happened between them and you that you gave that up.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Oh, I just don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And certainly the modern tendency.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, it is. But all the children that I went to school with were accustomed to having spankings at home. And many of the children that I taught were accustomed to having spankings at home too. So that was generally accepted as a method of discipline.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a teacher spank a kid in front of the whole class?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No. No. Now this man teacher that I mentioned, he equipped two boys for playing hooky one afternoon and he took them into the cloakroom as we call it. There was a boys' cloakroom and a girls' cloakroom and he took down his little buggy whip from behind the map case and led these boys one at a time out into the hall. And you could hear him spanking them and I'll tell you what put fear into all of us. But one thing I could say for this man, he could have avoided some of his disciplinary problems had he gone out on the playground with the boys and girls and played with them in their play period. And that's where the fight starts, you know, is over games and these children had all gone down on the ice skating and these two boys had stayed out all afternoon and had not returned to school whereas the rest of us had gone back to school. Well if he had walked down to the ice with us, even if he didn't have skates with him, but if he had just walked down to the ice and been with us while we were skating, you see that would never have happened. He'd have his bell, rung his bell and said, come on, we have to go back to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he didn't mix with the kids in their play period.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, he sat in the school house. And I know I avoided much, much disciplinary trouble by playing with the children and playing all the games I knew how. I was not a good baseball player, but I could play all the games like, oh, Dare base and Prisoner's base and any of those games where you ran and had fun. And that was good for the teacher as well as good for the children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Good for her?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Gave you your exercise, gave you your exercise and you let down the bars for a little while and just played as one of them. Then when the bell rang, things went right back into their ordinary positions again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you mean when you were on the playground with the kids you were no longer the teacher?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, I was in a way, but still I would take my place, take a place, the kids would choose me just as if I were another child and we played together and they might want me to settle disputes and I would do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't, you certainly didn't discipline them or you didn't boss them?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They did what they want?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yeah, we all let down, we all let down. Come on, yes, come on, we'll play. And they always loved to have the teacher play, even the big ones loved to have the teacher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it in the country schools?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: In the country schools it was, it was, yes. Although this man teacher that I had did not play with us, the lady before did not play with us. And, but the lady following the man teacher, she came out on the playgrounds and she played largely with the smaller children, the games that you play in circles and sing, she was a good singer. She sang, she didn't play so much the games of competition, more the circle games. And when I was in the, when I was in the eighth grade we had a teacher there that was real good to play with us. Real fast runner and play all the competitive games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, it does seem to me that for a lot of the country girls it was their kind of the chance to get off the farm to go and train to become a teacher and grow into a good one.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, that's true. And with the ones that took very little training, you know, it was a method of making a little money and bringing it back to the home too. Because that was a very difficult thing in the early days was to make a living. And that's why, now these men teachers that I mentioned, they had to support their family that way. And that is to help support the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you would say that a lot of that money that the teacher made went to the family instead of into her own pocket.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, yes. Now that's not true of all of them, but with a great many it was. But I do know some girls who were such spendthrifts and had such beautiful clothing and things that their parents even had to help them. They couldn't make it on their own with their job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even as teachers?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes. I think the first teachers that I heard of at Mount Tomer got about $40 a month. And then the teaching would only be maybe three months and then another period of three months. It wouldn't be more than six months. And then in later years when they got up to $100 a month and over $100 a month, that was very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that this playing on the playground for the kids that made them lose their fear of the teacher?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, it made them much, I don't know, it was very good. And some would say that you should keep yourself aloof and not be one of them. But no, you find your children minded you better, you had much better discipline if they got acquainted with you well on the school ground. And then you came in and you just told them if anybody started thinking it was still playtime, you told them this is school. They'd have to buckle right down again. But it improved the discipline and it kept all these fights from getting started out on the school ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hit kids when you taught? Did you have to use that? You never did?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No. Never used any hitting or spanking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you threaten them?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Maybe about once. Yeah, that I never had to spank them. And of course I had great backing from all the parents. They just backed me in everything that I tried to do. And that makes such a difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the teacher really brought culture to the community that she taught in? Or is that just a kind of a thing?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: I think that in the early days they certainly did. And the schools that I taught in, the people there were already quite cultured people. I taught at Mount Omer. I had many parents there who had been teachers. And many who were well educated and many, if not so well educated, were very intelligent, reading at home. They were exceptional people. And I don't think I could have brought them very much that they didn't already have. But still I could bring out things. For instance, there were quite a few Norwegian children there. And I could bring some of the customs of their country and what their fathers and mothers had done. And that was a kind of a contribution, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you taught them respect for their own culture?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, yes. And for the children who were not of that background, that gave them some idea of how things were done in a different country. And I know that when I was in the fourth grade, we had a teacher there who brought us things that I hadn't had. She sang very well, played the reed organ, taught us many, many nice songs. Children's songs, you know. And we sang and sang. And she taught us a little botany. None of us have had any botany. She taught us the parts of flowers and the names of flowers. She taught us... She taught us the site of music and art. She taught us quite a little bit of art that we never had any chance at. So she indeed brought culture with her into the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she older than most teachers?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, no. I believe she was 18 or not more than 20 and she had attended the normal school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was from normal school that she got it?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: From the normal school. I imagine she got a lot of it in her home. She probably had music lessons when she was a little girl. But she sang and she played.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about to the community at large? I mean beyond the children themselves. Not Mount Tomer, like you say. People were kind of cultured. Yes. But was it the same other places too? I know I've heard in some places that the school teacher really was a resource person for the community.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, I imagine that would be in earlier days than when I was doing the teaching. Now I taught at a school also called Lenville. And there in that community they had produced America's John McCormick. And that was a young man named Alfred Brigham. And he was a grand singer and learned at the University of Idaho. He went to Chicago and sang there in competition. 15 western states and Canada sent singers. And he was this lovely tenor and he was named America's John McCormick and first tenor. The next year this minister of music from Spokane sang in the same competition. He was a baritone and he was named second best baritone in the same group. So you see we had quite talented people down in that part of the country too. And there was a man there who had been a legislator down to Boise, lived in the Lenville area. So, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's interesting to me what you say about the culture of these people. Because it seems like they were putting themselves in a much more difficult situation economically compared to what perhaps they had grown up with with their parents. They grew up in a cultured home. Here they were out on the raw new land that had much more limited cultural opportunities. Wouldn't that be so?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, that is so. And I think they made quite a contribution there. And I know from the college they used to send out a lady who was heavily partnered. She'd come out and sing in the country schools. And my superintendent, Miss Ellen Peterson, would come out and play the reed organ. They'd have meetings in the evening and sing. And then there was a man who taught what he called a business college in Moscow. And through the wintertime he would have night school at school houses. And the farmers, adult men all, would come there and he would teach them forms of math that they could use on the farm. How to figure how much hay there wasn't a haystack. And how to figure how much there was a cord of wood and all these things that were very very useful to them. And how to write a business letter. And his name was Krikmure. And he ran Krikmure's business college in Moscow years ago. Then he would do this teaching at night of the adult men there. Also the University of Idaho would send out to some of the country schools a home ec lady and she would teach sewing. Maybe that would be on a Saturday and the women would come to the schoolhouse. So you see the schoolhouse was really quite the community center.
