speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be when you were growing up?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, this would be when I was teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That the women would come out to teach?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Right, right. That would be by the time I... Well, I guess a little before I was teaching too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To the adult women rather than to the girls?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, yes, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much quilting being done when you were growing up?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: No, no there wasn't. It was being done before I was growing up and it's being done again now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a child do you remember quilting?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, my mother never did it but I think there was some quilting and a lot of tying of quilts. Instead of all these little small stitches they would tie quilts. That was rather common when I was a girl and now the making of quilts is right back in the swing again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for your mother, Lola when she's talked to me about her has said that she really keenly felt the deprivation of the culture that she knew in Victoria.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, that's true. That's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that her isolation was a common experience for women in the rural areas here?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Yes, it was. Although she was there so early that she said the first year that she was there, you see she came in 1889. And the first year she was there she saw only one white woman and she came riding in on horseback. Otherwise any women she saw were Indian women. So then you would miss quite a bit. Although the Indian women had their own culture and they brought for her moccasins for all her boys. She could have learned bead work and things like that from them but they didn't even speak the English very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you talk about Indians. It makes me wonder about the kind of attitudes that people had in those days towards the Indians. How they looked at them. What do you think? I mean, do you think that they looked at them?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: In the time of my mother, now this would be before I was born, when she just had about four little boys, she was just a little bit afraid of them. Little bit afraid. But by the time I was in the grades I would see them at the fairs and riding through the country and we accepted them very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, and I know this is kind of a hard question, but do you think that the opinion of people at that time was that they were inferior?

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Well, no, I don't believe it. It was not with my family anyway. At the Mount Thomas School there was a girl who went there for a while who was partying then. And she was a very good student and got along just fine. Pretty well accepted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The mixing though was fair. There wasn't too much really, was there? I mean, it seems like most of the Indians did stay down on the reservation.

speaker: Elizabeth Gamble Wahl
words: Elizabeth was in a home, well sort of an adopted home, and was not with her parents. And she had come from Lewiston and was helping an elderly lady there with her work and then coming to school every day. She was a fifth or sixth grader. And it was company too for this lady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was asking Elizabeth about the attitudes that people had towards the Indians in the early days and the kind of, you know, what they felt about them.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I think my dad's feeling toward them in general was really pretty good. I had quite a bit of respect for them. Of course, I think any person probably would be struck by the difference in their feelings toward certain problems compared to, you know, two different social cultures. Yeah. Look at human problems quite differently sometimes. And this struck him, but he thought quite highly of the Nash Bush Indians here. And so highly that possibly if there had been no country school teachers, if the country school teachers had all been men, I wouldn't be surprised if I had turned out to be half Indian. This diary here contains a trip through Canada looking at land for a group of farmers in this country. And the partner that he had with him on this trip was married to an Indian and dad was very fond of this man's daughters. So that fits right into your question. Was this after he lived in Gen- well, it would have been after he lived in Gen- But he had also known this man a long time too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that in general in the community was there much- was there any looking down at the mixed marriages at that time?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: There were by some people, and it was resented by some people. And it was- I don't know. I went to school with people that were of mixed heritage. We thought real highly of them. One of the most popular families in our neighborhood was a mixed marriage. And I think the people felt more highly of the Indian side of the family than the other. Not because the other man had- not because the man had married an Indian. They just felt like the Indian family was kind of better people, nicer people, or easier to get along. There was this thing that I think probably led to a lot of this feeling that you expressed was the fact that the Indians did have this land. And a white man that married an Indian that had a nice allotment of land had an easier deal than the white man that married a white girl. And I think there was jealousy because of this difference right there, this money thing. There's always jealousy if the other guy has made some money and you haven't. And I think that might have had as much to do with it as anything else. But some of the Indians were highly thought of, quite capable people. They had different values. They didn't drive us hard for financial success. They valued some other things too. And eventually, I think practically all the Indians have turned out to just- they've found it to their advantage to rent their land out to white farmers. Although there are some that farm their own land. The early days a lot of them did. But as things got more complicated I guess, they didn't do that so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say they valued other things, what are you thinking of besides that?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, an Indian would think nothing of when it was time to go dig camas or hunt deer or go to a rodeo or a ball game. They're quite interested in athletics. They think nothing of leaving his farm no matter what critical time and going and taking his day off. That is the- I think they're aware that they financially wouldn't be ahead, but they also think that they'd rather do this other thing than do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the mixing of dances and that kind of thing? Was there any taboo about dancing?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Not in our dances. Not in our district. Of course there were only two families that came to our dances that were part Indian. Well, at one time I guess there was another family there. But, oh gee, they were popular people to dance with. No. There was this feeling about a lot of people. Some of the reservation Indians you felt were not as clean physically. And there was this feeling, alright, but the Indians in our neighborhood would, as far as housekeeping and so forth were concerned, they'd outdo most of the rest of us. So this would depend. But yes, you see our family was real friendly with the few Indians that were around. Thought real highly of them. And so we have also heard some of their feeling of the unfairness of the treatment that they've had. Our sympathies were with them. And yes, there were people that apparently would go out of their way to make them feel worse for having been Indian. Or for more so than that. Now one of our Indian friends, or our half-breed friends I should probably say, was telling my mother about how difficult it was to be of mixed blood. Because she said, the whites, the white people hate you and the Indians don't like you. So there was a problem. We of course didn't feel it and we didn't participate in it. But I think you'd have to say yes, by and large. Like all minorities, they haven't been dealt fairly with. I think it's probably better now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Although I have heard many people say that they feel highly towards Indians, that they like them very much. My dad did. And I almost wonder whether in some ways there might not have been more contact in those days between the races.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: There was. There was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I sometimes wonder about the stereotype.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Now some of the most pleasant memories I have are when an Indian man and his three-quarters Indian, I guess, son worked on the same combine that I did. And it was just a lot of fun. They were fun to work with. And nice. And we shared a lot of things that I would be much worse off not to have experienced. For instance, Indian words, Indian language. They're expression. They've got a keen sense of humor. Real keen. Real sharp. And also they're quite mechanically inclined. Quite ingenious. And when it comes to hardship, they're real tough. They're not complaining and crying about conditions that are tough. They acknowledge it being tough. But I mean it's with a sort of humor that you sort of respect. In the Indian language there aren't any swear words, as we know swear words. But it used to be quite a lot of fun then, instead of swearing about when, to use Indian words that had other meanings that still when translated into English would be swearing. And all this sort of helped pass the time and get over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an example of that?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No, it would be hard to bring it up because I'd have to say Indian words in Indian that it gets so involved, translating something that's funny in one language. It takes the two language to see the joke. Well I'll give you one little rough joke that we sort of enjoyed. In Indian, a possessive is usually formed by an appendage on the end of a word. And that word is often descriptive. But the Indian possessive is often heen. Now when they say a bull, when they try to say a bull, they'll use the word for cow and follow it by tala heen. Tala being an Indian word for testicle, meaning testicle. Or as we would say, ball. This animal which possesses balls, that would be the translation of the Indian word tala heen. Well this was real funny when we had trouble in the combine to use the Indian sentence that would say, we have lost our ball bearing. We ended with the word tala heen, which means bull. But anyhow, the Indians, they would chuckle over these things all the time. And of course they'd tell us the joke and we would too. And this was the kind of thing that went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tala heen was bull too. There wasn't another word that you had to use before tala heen?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well usually they would say a bull tala heen. That was enough if you're talking about cattle. And it would work just as if you're talking about sheep of course, it probably would work just as well too. Understandably enough, the word for rooster is not formed the same way at all. It's man chicken. Chicken man. Because you see, it wouldn't describe a rooster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that they expressed their feelings to your family, the feelings of mistreatment, that indicates to me that they must have felt pretty good about your family to be that frank.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh yes, I think so. I don't think they would say this to everybody, but they would say it to, well anybody that they considered a close friend I suppose. And of course their close friends wouldn't be the people that had mistreated them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of mistreatment do you think they went through? Would you be mistreated in the situation?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: The worst, well yes, I think that there was sometimes where they were taken advantage of, although I think that that is a knife that cut both ways. You know, that is to try to take financial advantage of one another. People do it between people of their own race too, you know, and the Indians are just as bad to the Indians as the whites were bad to the Indians, or the Indians to the Pines. But one of the things most resented was derogatory remarks or something. Although even that was taken with some humor. Now this same Indian that I speak of told me of an incident of somebody saying something to him. I guess he meant it as a joke, I don't know. But anyhow, I can't remember the exact words used, but the Dan Arthur, the Indian, was telling me about a man from Missouri who said to him something to the effect of, You damn cyewash, I'll knock your block off, or something like that. And Dan quoted himself as saying, alright you damn Missourian, come ahead. Now Dan told this, without resentment I think, mostly as a kind of a joke, and he sort of felt like he'd gone it on the Missourian because he'd used a worse word than cyewash. And cyewash was the worst word that you could use against an Indian because it means half-breed. Which bears out what this other person said, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For an Indian to marry a wife, was in some ways a loss of status for both in some ways?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I think so, yes I think so. In some ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The squaw man is the term that I would use to...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: That was the thing. I know of a falling out between two neighbors that never was healed just because of that. The white man had called the other white man who had married an Indian squaw, a squaw man, insulting him and insulting his wife. And a pretty serious thing too. And it was serious because the man was angry when he said it. Now I knew another man who felt a great deal of friendship for a man that was married to an Indian in the neighborhood there. So much so, these people were so well accepted, you know, you had to kind of guard yourself to remember that they were Indian. We didn't think of them as Indians, we thought of them differently. Well, this fellow always referred to his wife, his own wife, as the old squaw, just as a way of speaking. And he would commonly say to any other white man, well bring the old squaw over. Get the old squaws together. Well, he said, before he could stop himself, he said to this man who was married to an Indian, well bring the old squaw over some day, or tomorrow, or next year, something like that. And he said he no more than said it. And he just wished he could die. There's no way of undoing it. But so far as I knew or ever heard, there never was any resentment by the man when he said it to, because he understood it exactly as it was meant. Well, another example of how you can get into that kind of a trap, there was a Negro boy that worked for us. And he lived with us, in our house with us, and I liked him, I liked his brother. And he was telling us a thing that had happened to him when he was young. He and his brother, they were raised by their grandfather, their father had left and abandoned them I guess. But he and Stan, this was Laverne was the one that worked for us, and Stan was his brother. Tell him about one time he and Stan were out and it got into kind of a minor scrape like kids will. And Stan says, well, or I said to Stan, well should we tell grandpa? And Stan said, hell no, we're free white and 21. Well I had a hell of a time keeping a straight face. Verne I don't think ever noticed that he'd said it. Another time we were sitting at the table and I... We were talking about something or other that somebody had done, or some mistake a hired man had made, or something like that. Or something that he'd done that he shouldn't have done. And I said something to the effect, well Verne would certainly never make that mistake. And my mother said, well Verne is a white man. My mother never even realized what she'd said. I don't know that Verne did, nothing was ever said about it. I sure felt embarrassed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that those young fellows experienced any problems or words or anything while they were not from your family I'm sure? No. They would have stood out quite a bit.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, Laverne was always welcomed and treated real well by this Indian white family. I think he was treated equally well by others. But he always, I think, felt more easy with us, that is the Negro family, felt more easy with us and this Negro family than he did with some of the others. Of course this was back when Laverne, although he was a well liked student in high school and was a top athlete, and had lots of friends, and the family is an old established family, pioneer family. Yet, he couldn't get his hair cut in the daytime in a barber shop in Moscow anyway. One time he and I were eating in a restaurant in Coeur d'Alene and he said to me, you know if I wasn't with you they wouldn't have served me here. And I hadn't realized this, you know. But there were lots of restaurants where we could go. There was a Negro athlete in high school in Lewiston when I went there. This football team was in a neighboring town and they just treated this Negro swell. And there was another time, I think it was in a restaurant in Lewiston, where the football team went in to eat and they wouldn't serve the Negro so the whole team walked out. And this was not uncommon, although I hadn't realized it so much so until I had a few of these instances brought to my attention.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Vern a Lewiston boy?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No, he was in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, he was in Moscow. What's the last name?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Settles. Oh, I know Gene pretty well. He was Gene's nephew. He was at the memorial services this last decoration day, Vern wants. Of course the Settles, you know, by an awful lot of people. The Settles, people don't think of them as being Negroes, you know. An awful lot of people don't. Even people that are people that discriminate or think they do or feel like they do think of Settles as being a little different. Which I guess probably is true because they have intelligent people and they've adjusted. Now the Settles, as little boys, went to school with Elizabeth's brothers. But I don't know, things like that, they're getting better and better all the time I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've talked to Gene at length about what it was like for the family to have his family back in those days. And he has a great deal of pride.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, they are people that are intelligent enough, you know, to make a lot of room for other people. And overlook as nearly as you can, slights and so on. Because I'm sure they must have felt from one place to another. I was with Laverne in Coeur d'Alene one time, besides this restaurant thing that you mentioned where nothing bad happened. Where he was, this was during wartime, there was an army officer from South someplace. He really went out of his way to be nasty, to Laverne a perfect stranger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: World War II. You know, when Gene became warehouse superintendent for the grain growers, he said to me that it surprised a lot of people. And someone else told me, whose husband was on the board at the time of the grain growers, that he was fit to be died at the time when that happened. He was just quite disturbed, thinking it was very unfair. But to me, the fact that Gene got that position is very interesting because he was in charge of the lava white there.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, and probably, Gene being the kind of a man he is, probably the people that worked with and under him presented him less than the one that you speak of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Because they were nice people, they were just nice people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I think independent because of the fact that they were farmers, I think that was an important factor.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Kind of different? Well, he went to the home where Gene was raised, or that is, that his parents later got. It was one of the nicest homes in Moscow, and one of the nicest kept up and neat and clean. And Stanley, Laverne's brother, he had manners that just made me ashamed of myself. I mean, he was really polished.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, one of the things I want to ask you about was the change that went on through time as the farms began to become larger and as the families grew up. What I'm thinking of is the problem that I sense, which there was, by the fact that only a limited number of the boys could stay on the farm and become the operators. It seemed like things were going that way.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I think that's probably a problem. Of course, in our family, our family, as with generation after generation, got smaller and smaller. So it just fitted very well. But I have neighbors who have had several sons in the farm, and of course, they've either got to buy and acquire more land if they all want to be farmers, or else some of them have got to decide not to be farmers. One of the boys that worked for me for several years, all through most of his high school and most of his college years, he is one of three or four boys, I guess, about counting any of the girls. He took up agricultural engineering, and I don't know what his older brother did, but it wasn't farming. But I think the younger son is going to farm. Might very well be, though, that the younger son won't farm either. His dad is still doing the farming, and he's a man about my age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did the farms start to reduce in number? When did they begin to be larger and more consolidated?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, it's been going on ever since before the first homesteads were proved up on. A lot of people would come in and homestead a piece of land just for the sake of selling out their rights before they proved up on the homestead. I think it's a process that went on right from the beginning. My dad, operating his mother's farm, had an outfit to operate with. So, Annie had several brothers, so he had some source of labor. Paid for it, of course. But he just merely bought up land around there as the opportunities presented itself and bought the land and really accumulated more land than any of his brothers. Because they were all put together for that matter and never got any free land. But some of the first land he bought, I think he got from the neighborhood of somewhere between $16 and $20 a acre. But you must remember that in 1932 or 1933, some of that land right down there sold for about $34. That's depression. And some sold for $40. Over. In 1945, some choice land south of Moscow sold for the unheard of price. The sky high price of $150 a acre. That's within three miles of Moscow. And now, you hear of a land selling for $1500 a acre. So, what's land worth? More of a question of what's money worth. See, anytime you buy land, you've got to pay more than it looks like it would pay out. And if you don't buy land, you haven't got security because you could lose your lease in a hurry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's something that I... In fact, Gene Settle talked to me about... Who? Gene Settle talked about it among other people that in the 1920s, as people started going to gas machinery, there was a big squeeze on renters who didn't have it. Hmm, horses. Do you think that's an accurate...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I think probably I should have answered your question this way, that of course the big incentive toward owning more land had to come when you had easier ways of farming, bigger pieces of land. Of course, with the tractors and with the advanced machinery and so on. And with the acquiring of capital, too, the ability to finance purchase of this kind of machinery when it became available.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you... In your family, when did the tractors first...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: We bought our first tractor in 1930. And it was owned by my father and a brother in partnership. We still used horses at the same time, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the tractor make a big difference in the way of the immediate one?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah. You could... Not only you could farm more land, but you could farm it faster and better. You know that, and do a better job of it. Raise more, produce more. Well, one thing, you... Think of the land that you had to devote to just producing feed for the horses that you worked. Took quite a lot of effort. And doing your work at the right time made a lot of difference. Another thing... There are always shorter horses when you needed them, and they had too many when you didn't have any work for them. Tractors, when you get done in the fall, you just put them in the shed. The horses, you've got to work for them always. Of course, you could... You know, a lot of the year they could gather their own feed. If you were lucky enough to have some inexpensive pasture land that would help you winter them over, that was really a pretty good thing. Good for the horses, too, because dry feed isn't the best way to maintain animals all the time, either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, before I go, I wanted to ask you, and it's something that Mary Edwards said to me about your mother, that after your brother that was spastic was born, that she really had to devote a great deal of her effort to take care of him.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, yeah, she did. Of course. Yeah, she... But of course, you must understand that he was able to do quite a few things. He learned to drive a car, he could take her any place she wanted to go, and he kept the books and the records for the operation there, and did the banking, ran a lot of the errands. But he had to be dressed and put to bed and cleaned and cared for, you know. And it was... I never referred to complaining about it, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like the kind of thing that would make you have more compassion for other people, to have that in your own family.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, yes, you do. But, you know, there's also... there's something else that goes along with it, too. And I suppose I would be dishonest if I didn't admit it, and I think probably most people would, too. You have this compassion, all right, but also it takes any pleasure that you might otherwise have, it kind of destroys it for you that the less fortunate one can't enjoy it with you. Or also, there's a feeling of guilt, any pleasure, say it me, I just will say, any pleasure that I might have, there's always a feeling of guilt that accompanies that. Still, you have the... there's a lot of things that you feel like you ought to have a right to do without being handicapped by the fact that your brother is a cripple. Why should I be crippled just because my brother is a cripple? Why should I act as a cripple? Why should I have to take it every place I go, you know, and yet you feel guilty if you don't? Now, there's this aspect of it, too. So I don't know what kind of an answer to give you on that. I think probably I'm compassionate in a way, and still having that experience, I think it's almost inevitable that maybe you develop a sort of a numbness or a lack of conscience. You also have this feeling of guilt that goes along with it, too. So I don't know. You look back, you know, now that he's dead, you look back and you think of all the things that little sacrifices that I might have made that would have made it better for him, you feel ashamed that you didn't. And yet were I back there, I doubt if I would have the character to act any better than I did. Now, I don't know whether I can explain it any better than that or not, but that is a part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You probably have a very strong feeling of protectiveness.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh yes, and it starts from before you can even remember. So, you fight with a crippled brother the same as you'd have to fight with any other brother, and they're no better, no more of an angel than you are either. Maybe not as much. He was really pretty good. My parents never, he was never spoiled, you know, because of being unfortunate. I thought this was a pretty difficult thing for my parents to do, but they tried to be as just to the weld kids as they would be to the crippled one. And of course the crippled kid has to be punished once in a while, too, and that must be pretty tough on a parent. Oh, we had lots of fun together, too, you know, lots of things to enjoy. He was a generous, very generous brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, Mary Edwards really remembers that your mother as being the ideal homemaker, housekeeper, feels that your house was the most outstanding one of all of them as far as how clean it was, how nice it was, the feeling, the atmosphere, and that's how she remembered it.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Did Mary ever tell you how she keeps her house? Well, I imagine that she put my mother to shame, really. Her house was splendid, too. If my mother was, you know, I think kids sort of pick an ideal older person to sort of pattern themselves after. And speaking of my mother the way she did, why, I really think that if my mother was her example, why she outdid her example. Mary did. Lovely housekeeper. Always neat. Always made you welcome in her house, too. Same as my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that was in itself a real important value of people at that time? The pride in the kind of house they kept?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Oh, yes. It was a sort of a plume that a lot of people held up, too, you know, because it was quite difficult. Now, just to show you how difficult, you heard Mary describe the kind of a housekeeper my mother was. Well, I overhauled an automobile on the floor of her living room one winter. So she didn't sacrifice everything to the housekeeping. And many of the time we've had a baby pig in the house to fill it out. All the chickens that we dressed, you know, part of the dressing was done in the house on the kitchen table. And so, I, the house, it's part of the farm, you know, the farmhouse. And still, Mother managed to keep a pretty nice house. She took quite a pride when she was young enough and strong enough that when the men came in from the field, that the table looked nice and there was a white tablecloth on the table. And the table was set nice, everything was right. Yeah, I think that's right. I think the pioneer women took quite a pride in their house. It was the only thing they had, really, their house. Oh, of course, people took pride in their horses too. But the women. Maybe more the house. More the house. The house was the woman's. They weren't very many of them able to keep much of a lawn or yard, you know, they would have liked to. But the water was, there wasn't any, there were very few pressure water systems, and by pressure I mean gravity water systems. Windmills, of course, you had water when the wind blew, you pumped it and carried it in a bucket when it didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there common windmills at that time?

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: No, I would say they were a little less than common. I think most people pumped and carried the water in a bucket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what you're saying is that it was really a struggle for a woman to be able to keep the house clean with a lack of water and...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Now my mother was a little bit lucky in this respect. She had lived in town some, and her father was a tinner and was a pretty good plumber. And my dad was pretty ingenious too. And so as far back as I can remember there was a water system in every house that I lived in. Although it wasn't always in working order still, there was plumbing there inside plumbing. Of course part of that might have been because of that, my brother was crippled. But we also had outdoor plumbing too, as everybody did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I really should get going. It's about 1030 I see. I've been here quite a while.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Well, I don't think I've helped you much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wouldn't want to disagree with you, but I'm...

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: I always thought of this country where I first discovered myself as being an old established country. And the older I get, when I look back and get a better perspective on it, I see how close to the edge of the pioneering I was here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Yeah, when I was born it was only 31 years after the Wall people had moved into this country. And I'm 65 years old, yeah sure. But when I was about 10 years old, God it seemed to me like the world I found myself in must have been going on about like that forever. And still I can remember fairly respectable caravans of Indians coming by our place on the way to the mountains to pick huckleberries. Or whatever they went there for. I don't remember ever seeing any wild horses, but I suppose the last of them must have been around somewhere very shortly before I was born.

speaker: Tom Wahl
words: Maybe even while I was still here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I understand...
